steps.title: Identify the grammatical structures
steps.content: On the left: “Scott-Heron himself resisted the godfather” forms a complete independent clause (once “nickname” is added as the object). On the right: “feeling that it didn't encapsulate his devotion...” is a participial phrase.

steps.title: Apply the parenthetical punctuation rule
steps.content:
Parenthetical words that interrupt a sentence must be enclosed by commas on both sides, preserving the unity of the sentence.

Choice C — nickname, however, places commas around “however,” to enclose it as a parenthetical interrupter.

The other choices all fail this rule: one lacks the comma after “however,” one uses an illogical semicolon mid-punctuation, and one uses a semicolon to incorrectly treat the following participial phrase as a second independent clause.

That matches Choice C — nickname, however,.
